coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of dopamine using phthalocyanine-nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot conjugates
coredata.dc:creator: Ndebele, Nobuhle
Sen, Pinar
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Two cobalt phthalocyanine molecules were synthesized, a symmetrically substituted CoPc (1) and an unsymmetrically substituted CoPc (2). These CoPcs were π-π stacked onto nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). The CoPcs, NGQDs and their respective conjugates were used as electro-catalytic probes in the electrochemical oxidation of dopamine. The molecules were adsorbed onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) using the drop dry method, the GCE was used to support those catalysts. The electrooxidation of dopamine was studied using cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry. An improvement in the sensitivity and limit of detection was observed upon conjugation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Nitrogen-doped quantum dots
Electrocatalyst
Cobalt phthalocyanine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of phthalocyanines onto NGODs Scheme (2)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of (a) NGQDs in water, 1 and 1@NGQDs, (b) NGQDs, 2 and 2@NGQDs. NGQDs samples were run in water while the Pcs and conjugates were in DMF.
FT-IR spectra for compounds 1, 2 and A.
Raman spectra illustrating the changes in the D and G bands of NGQDs, 1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs.
DLS measurements illustrating the change in particle size of NGQDs, 1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs upon conjugation.
Cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrodes and bare electrode in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in 0.1 M KCl supporting electrolyte at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1.
Approach curves of the modified electrodes, the bare glassy carbon plate and non-conducting Teflon. All in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in 0.1 M KCl.
Cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrodes and bare electrode (insert) in 1 mM dopamine in 0.1 M PBS buffer at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1.
Cyclic voltammogram stability scans for a) GCE/1 and b) GCE/1@NGQDs in 1 mM dopamine in 0.1 M PBS. At a scan rate of 100 mVs−1.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms for the detection of 1 mM dopamine (in 0.1 M PBS) at different scan rates, (b) plot of oxidation peak vs log v. and (c) plot of peak current vs. square root of the scan rate. Electrode = GCE/1@NGQDs.

(a) Chronoamperometric scans of GCE/1@NGQDs at different dopamine concentration in 0.1 M PBS. Insert current vs. concentration plot for hydrazine. (b) Plot of (Icat/Ibla) vs t1/2. (c) plot of the square of the slope vs. concentration of dopamine.
Representation of the synthesis of compounds 1 and 2.
Representation of the conjugation of 1 onto NGQDs yielding 1@NGQDs (as a representative).
Summary of characterisation data observed upon conjugation. UV–Vis spectra recorded in DMF unless otherwise stated.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Absorption band and not the Q band of the NGQDs in water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the probe properties for the detection of 1 mM dopamine unless otherwise stated.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of dopamine using phthalocyanine-nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot conjugates

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two cobalt phthalocyanine molecules were synthesized, a symmetrically substituted CoPc (1) and an unsymmetrically substituted CoPc (2). These CoPcs were π-π stacked onto nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). The CoPcs, NGQDs and their respective conjugates were used as electro-catalytic probes in the electrochemical oxidation of dopamine. The molecules were adsorbed onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) using the drop dry method, the GCE was used to support those catalysts. The electrooxidation of dopamine was studied using cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry. An improvement in the sensitivity and limit of detection was observed upon conjugation.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallophthalocyanines (MPc) are eighteen π electron-rich macrocyclic molecules [1,2], which have been extensively researched for a wide variety of applications such as photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy (PDT) and degradation of pollutants, in nonlinear optics, in fuel cells and in bioimaging to name a few [2–7]. Over the years these molecules have been extensively employed as electrocatalysts since they exhibit remarkable redox activities and good physicochemical properties and stability [8,9]. MPcs owe their electroactivity to the electro-active central metal ions (such as cobalt, nickel, iron and manganese) as well as the substituents around the core of the phthalocyanine molecule [1,2,10,11]. MPcs have a planar structure that allows them to undergo reversible redox reactions and retain full stability and functionality [12–14]. In this work, cobalt phthalocyanines are employed as electrocatalysts in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

GQDs are a two-dimensional, carbon-based monolayer sheet formed by sp2 bonded carbon atoms [15]. GQDs have been considered for various applications including but not limited to photovoltaics, photodetectors, catalysis, in vitro and in vivo imaging and electrochemical sensing [16–21]. Similar to MPcs, GQDs possess good electronic properties and have been employed as electrocatalysts. GQDs enhance the electrocatalytic activity of phthalocyanine molecules [13].

In this work, we combine the two electrocatalysts; MPcs and nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) for enhanced electrocatalysis by synergistic effect towards the detection of dopamine. Two CoPc derivatives are employed in this work: one is tetrasubstituted with diamino functional groups (compound 1), hence symmetrically substituted; the other with three diamino functional groups and a carboxyphenoxy (mono) group (compound 2), hence unsymmetrically substituted. The CoPc derivatives were adsorbed onto NGQDs using π-π stacking. Both MPcs and GQDs possess π electrons making π-π stacking possible. Complex 2 contains push (dimethylamino) and pull (COOH) substituents. Push-pull substituents enhance electron transfer properties of complexes [22].

These conjugates were then adsorbed onto the glassy carbon electrode and further used for the electrochemical detection of dopamine as a test analyte. Cobalt was selected as the metal of choice since CoPc derivatives exhibit remarkable electrocatalytic activity [23–26].

FePc complexes have been studied in the presence of GQDs for the electrocatalysis of oxygen reduction reaction [27]. We have also reported on the electrocatalytic behaviour of asymmetrical push–pull CoPcs in the presence of GQDs [28,29] with good electrocatalytic activity. Asymmetry improved electrocatalytic activity [28]. The CoPc derivative employed in literature [28] contained three diethylamino groups, in this work we double the number of amino groups by using bis-(dimethylamino)2-propyloxy] substituents (Scheme 1 ), the presence of more electron-rich nitrogen groups may enhance the electrocatalytic activity of the Pc. The ring system used in this work has been reported for Ni [30], but it is used for the first time with Co as the central metal. GQDs employed in this work are doped with nitrogen (to form NGQDs) since it has been reported that nitrogen-doped graphitic materials containing pyridinic nitrogen have high electrocatalytic activity [31].

Dopamine (DA) is an important neurotransmitter that plays a role in the functioning of the human metabolism, central nervous, hormonal, renal and cardiovascular system [32]. Dopamine deficiency can lead to Parkinson’s disease, and abnormal dopamine concentration can lead to Schizophrenia and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder [33], hence the importance of its detection. Dopamine has been detected using MPc complexes in the presence of other nanomaterials [34], but not GQDs.

Tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBABF4), cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (OAc)₂·4H₂O), 1,8-diazobicyclo [5.4.0] undec-7-ene (DBU), and alumina nanopowder (<10 µm) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 1-Pentanol, dimethylformamide (DMF) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Merck and dried before use. Type II Elga water was obtained from an Elga PURE LAB Chorus 2 (Ro/di) system. 4-((1,3-Bis(dimethylamino)propan-2-yl)oxy)phthalonitrile (A) [30], 4-(4-carboxyphenoxy)phthalonitrile (B) [35] and nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) [36] were prepared as reported in literature.

Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2250 spectrophotometer. The mass spectra data were measured on a Bruker AutoFLEX III Smart beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer, an α-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid was selected as the MALDI matrix while a 355 nm Nd: YAG laser was used as the ionizing source. Infrared spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 Fourier Transform-Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. The Raman data was collected using the Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector). Sizes of the NGQDs and conjugates were determined using the dynamic light scattering (DLS) principle on a Malvern Zetasizer nano series, Nano-ZS90.

All electrochemical experimental studies (cyclic voltammetry, CV, and chronoamperometry) were performed using Autolab potentiostat PGSTAT 302 electrochemical workstation (driven by GPES software version 4.9). A three-electrode electrochemical cell comprising of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode and silver|silver chloride wire (Ag|AgCl) (3 M KCl) as reference electrode was used. While the scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) experiments were carried out using Uniscan Model 370 equipment and a 10 µm Pt microelectrode (Uniscan) as the tip. SECM approach curves were obtained using the Pt microelectrode with a Pt counter electrode and Ag|AgCl wire as the pseudo-reference electrode. Glassy carbon plates (Goodfellow, UK) of 1 × 1 cm and 2 mm thick were used as substrates for the SECM measurements.

Compound A (200 mg, 0.734 mmol) and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (91 mg, 0.367 mmol) were dissolved in dry 1-pentanol (4 mL) in the presence of DBU (0.5 mL), the reaction mixture was heated at reflux temperature (140˚C) for 18 h under inert atmosphere and thereafter allowed to cool to room temperature. The resultant product was centrifuged in DMSO serval times and centrifuged in water and thereafter washed in ethanol several times, the supernatant was dried under vacuum.

Compound 2 was obtained by mixing 4-((1,3-bis(dimethylamino)propan-2-yl)oxy)phthalonitrile (A) (250 mg, 0.918 mmol) with 4-(4-carboxyphenoxy) phthalonitrile (B) (80 mg, 0.306 mmol and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (114 mg, 0.459 mmol) in 1-pentanol (4 mL) in the presence of DBU (0.5 mL). The mixture was refluxed for 18 h under an inert atmosphere. The blue product was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged in DMSO several times. The product was dried under vacuum and further purified using silica column chromatography with chloroform, and gradually increasing the polarity of the eluent system with ethyl acetate up to 5% yielding compound 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of phthalocyanines onto NGODs Scheme (2)
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesised compounds 1 and 2 were adsorbed onto the NGQDs using the non-covalent, π-π stacking method. The procedure previously reported in the literature was modified slightly for this work [37]. Compounds 1 and 2 (10 mg, 8.71 × 10-3 mmol or 8.77 × 10-3 mmol for 2) were dissolved in 2 mL DMF. NGQDs (5 mg) in water (1 mL) were added to the reaction vessel and sonicated at room temperature for 24 h. The reaction mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 96 h. The formed conjugated products (1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs) were precipitated and washed in ethanol, then dried under vacuum.

The GCE surface was cleaned on a Buehler-felt polishing pad in a slurry comprising of alumina nanopowder (<10 µm) and purified water. The electrode was then rinsed several times with Elga purified water.

The electrodes were modified using the drop-dry method. When modifying the NGQDs alone onto the GCE, 5 µL of the NGQDs solution in water (1 mg/ mL) were deposited on the electrode surface and dried overnight in an oven at 60˚C, the electrode is represented as GCE/NGQDs. For compound 1, 2 and the conjugates, solutions of 1 mg of each in 1 mL DMF were prepared and the electrodes modified using the same procedure followed for NGQDs (5 µL of each was used). The electrodes are represented as GCE/1, GCE/2, GCE/1@NGQDs and GCE/2@NGQDs for compounds 1 and 2 their conjugates, respectively.

Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis of a symmetrical phthalocyanine molecule (1) as well as the synthesis of an asymmetrical phthalocyanine molecule (2). Compound 1 was synthesised through the conventional cyclotetramerization method [38], using a ratio of 2:1 of the phthalonitrile (A) to cobalt acetate. This allows for the complete cyclization of the phthalonitrile and metalation, avoiding the formation of metal-free Pcs, thus eliminating the need for column chromatography [39]. Whereas compound 2 was formed using the stoichiometric ratio of 3:2 between the two phthalonitriles, this was to ensure very little to no formation of tetrasubstituted phthalocyanines [39]. Cyclotetramerization of both compounds 1 and 2 was achieved in the presence of 1-pentanol, DBU and cobalt acetate as the metal of choice. The synthesis of the compounds was confirmed by; UV/Vis, FTIR and mass spectrometers, and elemental analysis. The elemental analysis results are in agreement with observations that some Pcs are isolated as hydrates [40]. 1H NMR spectra were not recorded due to the paramagnetic nature of the central metal.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to further confirm the formation of the compounds, Fig. 2 . The disappearance of the nitrile stretch observed at 2248 cm−1 for phthalonitrile (A) spectra from the spectra of compounds 1 and 2 shows successful cyclization of the phthalonitrile and the formation of the two Pcs. The two Pcs have very similar FT-IR spectra. For compound 1 (2 in brackets), a stretch associated with the aromatic CH bonds were observed at 3106 cm−1 (3100 cm−1) and the aliphatic CH stretch was observed between 2928 cm−1 and 2860 cm−1 for both compounds. The aromatic carbon–oxygen bond stretches were observed at 1200 cm−1 and 1064 cm−1 (1254 cm−1 and 1088 cm−1). Another stretch at 1591 cm−1 (1520 cm−1) attributed to the CC bond was observed. Compound 2 showed a further stretch at 1659 cm−1 and this was attributed to the CO bond of the carboxylic acid.

Various methods can be employed for the adsorption (conjugation) of quantum dots onto porphyrinoids [42]. Quantum dots can be easily conjugated to such molecules due to their structure (they are monolayer π conjugated sheets). For this work, the nitrogen-doped quantum dots were conjugated to the respective phthalocyanine molecules via π-π stacking as shown in Scheme 2 . The loading of compounds 1 and 2 onto NGQDs was estimated methods previously described in literature using the absorption spectra [43]. This involves the qualitative comparison of the Q band absorbance intensity of the Pc in the conjugate and before conjugation. The loading was similar for the two compounds, at 0.5 mg Pc/mg NGQDs

Graphene quantum dots typically have well-established absorption bands in the UV region due to the π-π transitions of the sp2 domains [44,45]. The absorption peak of quantum dots depends on the method used in their preparation and not their size [45,46]. The quantum dots synthesised in this work have an absorption peak at 348 nm (Fig. 1).

It has been reported that the Q band absorptions of Pcs are sensitive to π-π interactions and that the strong π-π interactions result in the formation of complexes between Pcs and nanomaterials in the ground state hence spectral shifts are observed upon conjugation [47]. Fig. 1a and b (Table 1) illustrate the absorption spectra for 1 and 2 and their respective conjugates (1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs). A 10 nm blue shift in the Q band was observed for compound 1 upon conjugation. While the opposite 16 nm red shift was observed upon the conjugation of compound 2. Red shifts have been attributed to the flattening of porphyrins in porphyrins-graphene oxide nanoconjugates [48,49]. However, blue spectral shifts have also been reported for porphyrin-graphene oxide conjugates [49]. These spectral changes are attributed to strong interactions between the porphyrinoid and the graphitic sheet. The increase in the intensity of the B band observed upon conjugation is attributed to the intense absorption of the NGQDs. Apart from spectral shifts, there is broadening of the Q bands for both compounds suggesting aggregation.

Raman spectroscopy provides data that proves the change in the graphene framework of the NGQDs, if any, upon conjugating to Pcs, Fig. 3 . The G band peaks observed in the Raman spectra are attributed to the in-plane vibrations of the sp2 bonded carbon atoms. While the D band peaks are due to the out-of-plane vibrations caused by structural defects, sp3 carbon atoms. The NGQDs alone showed G and D band peaks at 1605 cm−1 and 1171 cm−1 respectively. Upon conjugation, there is no change in the G band of 2@NGQDs and a 12 cm−1 change in the G band of 1@NGQDs. However, there no change was observed in the D band of 1@NGQDs and a ≈20 cm−1 blue shift was observed for 2@NGQDs. Shifts in the G band indicate strong π interactions between a porphyrinoids and the coplanar graphitic sheet, indicating that a hybrid complex has been formed due to charge transfer between the two [50,51]. The lack of change in the D band of 1@NGQDs indicates that the central graphene sheet was intact during π-π conjugation and that the structural integrity was preserved. Shifts have also been reported to indicate a change in size [52], and strain effect on nanoparticles [52,53]. Upon conjugation, the order of the graphene framework is disrupted and an increase in the ID/IG ratio is expected [54]. The ID/IG ratio for NGQDs alone was determined to be 0.24, upon conjugation an increase in the ratio was observed, Table 1. The ID/IG ratios were calculated to be 0.80 and 0. 67 for 1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs respectively.

Dynamic light scattering was used to determine the average particle size (Table 1) of the NGQDs and their respective conjugates, Fig. 4 . The average NGQDs size was determined to be 4.96 nm. The size as expected, increased when conjugated to the respective Pc molecules with average sizes of 19.2 nm and 23.5 nm (Table 1) for 1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs respectively. The increase in particle size upon conjugation confirms the possible supramolecular assembly formation.

Zeta potential data aids in the determination of the stability and colloidal dispersions [55]. Zeta potential is the measure of charge carried by articles when they are suspended in solution [55]. The magnitude of the zeta potential indicates the degree of electrostatic repulsions between the adjacent and similarly charged particles in dispersion [55]. Particles with a high zeta potential (irrespective of whether it is positive or negative) are said to be relatively stable and are therefore not prone to aggregation [56]. A small zeta potential indicates that the molecules are relatively unstable and thus attractive forces may exceed their repulsion and may result in aggregation [56]. The zeta potential of the NGQDs was determined to be −26.1 mV in water, Table 1. This value is relatively higher than that of compounds 1 and 2 and their respective conjugates, indicating that NGQDs alone are very stable. Table 1 shows that the NGQDs alone and the conjugates are relatively more stable than the Pcs alone.
The working electrode was modified using the drop-dry adsorption method, where the Pcs and conjugates were adsorbed onto the electrode and allowed to dry in the oven overnight before use.

Fig. 5 is an illustration of the cyclic voltammograms of the bare electrode as well as the modified electrodes in 1 mM ferricyanide solution ([Fe(CN)6]3-/4-) with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte. It is observed that the various electrodes employed in this work have different peak potential separations (ΔEp) (Table 2 , Fig. 5a) which is an indication that they each have varying charge transfer capabilities in the ferrocyanide solution [57,58]. A lower ΔEp is highly desired in electrochemistry as it indicates that the electrode has good electron transfer abilities, however, this does not directly translate to the electron transfer properties of the probes observed in the presence of the analyte. The following conclusions can be drawn with regards to the bare and modified electrodes:
The bare electrode had a relatively lower ΔEp value (64 mV) compared to the modified probes. Hence the bare GCE has good charge transfer ability.

The synthesised phthalocyanine molecules had better electron transfer abilities relative to their respective conjugates. With 2 having a ΔEp of 107 mV which is larger than for 1 at 170 mV, showing the importance of low symmetry. GCE/2@NGQDs only displayed an oxidation peak and no reduction peak was observed in the window studied, and therefore its peak separation potential could not be determined. The large (or lack of return peak) charge separation for MPc@NGQDs, could be due to the fact that ferrocene readily adsorbs on the carbon nanomaterials [59] hence resulting in low or lack of reversibility. Ferrocene has π electrons hence will readily adsorb on other π containing materials. There are more π electrons on the conjugates, hence there is enhanced adsorption of ferrocene, and lack of reversibility.
In terms of improving charge transfer behaviour, the order is as follows: GCE > GCE/2 > GCE/NGQDs > GCE/1 > GCE/1@NGQDs, ignoring GCE/2@NGQDs since we could not determine its ΔEp.
Although GCE has better electron transfer capabilities in the ferrocyanide solution, it does not mean that it will have better dopamine detection abilities.

The scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) technique was used to characterize the bare and modified electrodes. The approach curves obtained (in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte) are illustrated in Fig. 6 , where d/a is the normalised distance and i/iLim is the normalised current.

The approach curves illustrate the changes in tip current as the ultra-micro electrode (UME) tip approaches the substrate in a feedback mode experiment in the presence of Fe3+/Fe2+ redox mediator. The UME has a hemispherical diffusion layer in the solution [60,61]. As the electrode is moved closer to the surface and the UME is approached, the hemispherical layer changes into a “convection style” redox reaction which increases current, if the surface is conductive. It can be observed that the current increases as the tip approaches the bare electrode (GCE) and hence this demonstrates that the electrode is a conductor [60,61]. The Teflon surface is insulating, non-conductive, therefore it hinders diffusion resulting in a decrease in current as is observed in Fig. 6. The Pcs and their conjugates are less conducting when compared to bare GCE confirming the CV results. There was no obvious trend observed in the insulating behaviour of the electrodes.

The general mechanism for the oxidation of dopamine is described as the ECE (electron transfer, chemical reaction, electron transfer) (Scheme 3 ) [62,63]. Dopamine exists is a cationic form in neutral environments [34]. Its electrochemical oxidation occurs via a two proton and two-electron process yielding o-dopaminoquinone (o-DQ), this step is known as the E step and is reversible. The chemical reaction, C step, occurs under neutral or basic conditions, whereby the amine group in o-DQ is deprotonated and cyclization occurs forming leucodopaminochrome (LDC). LCD is further oxidised under by the two-electron, two proton process at relatively lower potentials resulting in the formation of dopaminochrome (DC).

Cyclic voltammetry was used to determine the electrolytic behaviour of the probes in the presence of dopamine. The effectiveness of the probes in the detection of dopamine is illustrated in Fig. 7 . All modified electrodes showed both oxidation and reduction peaks as reported in literature [33]. The probes displayed oxidation peak potentials ranging between 0.21 V and 0.54 V (Table 2). The following conclusions can be drawn from the peak potentials obtained from the oxidation of dopamine.
i)
All probes showed oxidation peaks, with the exception of the bare electrode.
ii)
GCE/1 gave a lower potential compared to GCE/2, showing no advantage of asymmetry. The same applies to GCE/1@NGQDs compared to GCE/2@NGQDs.
iii)
The oxidation peak potential obtained for NGQDs was significantly high.
iv)
The combination of GQDs with Pcs resulted in the shifting of the oxidation peak potentials to less positive values compared to Pc or NGQDs alone, with GCE/1@NGQDs displaying the lowest oxidation peak potential. The observed superior behaviour of compound 1 and its conjugates suggest the lack of symmetry in 2 is not as important as the presence of more nitrogen groups in the former.

In relation to the peak catalytic currents obtained for the oxidation of dopamine, GCE/1@NGQDs and GCE/2 had the highest current equally. GCE/2@NGQDs had the lowest current. There was no obvious trend observed with regards to the currents, no indication as to where conjugation aids in improving the catalytic current.

For a sensor to be regarded and used practically, it needs to be stable and consequently produce reproducible results. In this work stability studies of the probes in 1 mM dopamine (0.1 M PBS) were conducted by running 20 scans consecutively (Fig. 8 ). The stability of probes is generally characterized by a decrease in the peak current with the increase in the number of scans conducted. A decrease in the peak current was observed for GCE/1 (Fig. 8a) after the first scan, however, the electrode stabilized after the 10th scan and very little change in the peak current was observed, a slight shift in the peak potential was also observed. An increase in the peak current was observed for GCE/1@NGQDs (Fig. 8b) and no change was observed in the oxidation peak potential. Thus, NGQDs improve the stability of the electrode towards the detection of dopamine.

The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the peak currents in the stability studies were calculated to determine the precision and the reproducibility (stability) of the electrocatalysts studied in this work. The RSD values calculated ranged between 2.05% and 7.64%. The overall decrease in peak current was below 40%. All probes used in this work proved to be stable and therefore could be used several times without the respective peaks disappearing completely or the oxidation peak potentials changing indicating that the results are reproducible

Kinetic studies for the detection of dopamine were conducted at varying scan rates (10 mVs−1 to 100 mVs−1) and fixed dopamine concentration to determine the reversibility of the catalysed reaction and to additionally determine the Tafel slopes of the studied probes. Fig. 9 (a) shows the cyclic voltammograms of GCE/1@NGQDs at the different scan rates. The oxidation peak potential slightly shifts towards more positive values (increases) as the scan rate increases. It can also be observed that the peak current also increases with the increase in the scan rate. A similar pattern was observed in relation to the increase in oxidation peak potential and current with an increase in the scan rate for all the probes studied.

The linear relationship between the oxidation peak current and the square root of the scan rate (Fig. 9c) shows that the oxidation of dopamine is predominantly diffusion-controlled [64]. A similar pattern was observed for all the probes studied in this work suggesting that the oxidation of dopamine using the GCE/1, GCE/2@NGQDs, GCE/2, GCE/2@NGQDs and GCE/NGQDs was diffusion controlled.

In order to determine the Tafel slopes of the probes, Eq. (1) was used [65].
(1)
E p
=
b 2
log v + K
where b is the Tafel slope, Ep represents the oxidation peak potential, V is the scan rate and K is the constant. A linear relationship between the oxidation peak potential and the scan rate was observed (Fig. 9b). Plots obtained for all probes were linear. Tafel slopes within the 60 mV – 120 mV range are associated with a one-electron transfer process, during the rate-determining step when α = 1 [66–68]. The Tafel slopes obtained for this work range between 144 mV and 300 mV (Table 2). Tafel slopes higher than 120 mV/decade have no kinetic meaning and are due to absorption on the electrode surface. High Tafel slopes values are associated with the superimposition of two or more electron transfer reactions, which is the case with the electrooxidation of dopamine as illustrated in Scheme 3, high values are attributed to the by-products from the two-step reaction being absorbed at the electrode surface [69,70]. These high values are an indication of tight binding between the substrate (dopamine) and the catalyst (synthesised molecules) hence they are attributed to adsorption at the electrode surface [69,70].

Chronoamperometry was used to determine parameters such as the catalytic rate constants, sensitivity and the limit of detection (LoD) of the probes towards the detection of dopamine. Fig. 10 a is an illustration of the chronoamperogram evolutions when varying the concentration of dopamine for GCE/1@NGQDs as an example. While the insert shows the linear relationship between the concentration of dopamine and the corresponding current. Using this plot, the LoDs of all the probes were determined (Table 3 ), Eq. (2) was used to determine the LOD.
(2)
LoD =
3 δ
S
where δ and S are the standard deviation (of the blanks) and slope of the calibration curve respectively. The LoDs obtained for the conjugates, GCE/1@NGQDs and GCE/2@NGQDs were relatively lower than those of their respective unconjugated Pcs showing the importance and significance of conjugating. The LoDs obtained in this work are an improvement compared to literature values based on other phthalocyanines (Table 3) [34].

Fig. 10b represents the ratio between the current at various concentrations (Icat)and that of the buffer/blank (Ibla) against the square root of the time. The gradients obtained from the plots were used to determine the catalytic rate constant (k) of the respective probes towards dopamine. The catalytic rate constant was determined according to a method described in literature, and Eq. (3) [71,72].
(3)
I
cat
I
bla
=
y
1 2
π
1 2
=
π
1 2
k
C
t
1
2
where C the concentration of dopamine and t the time that has elapsed in seconds. Plotting the square of the slopes from Fig. 10b) against their respective concentrations gave linear plots (Fig. 10c). The slope (equal to πk where k is the rate constant) was used to calculate the catalytic rate constant for the probes studied. The plots are represented by eqs. (4)(i) to (v):
(4)
y = s l o p e
d o p a m i n e c o n c e n t r a t i o n
s
- 1
mM
+ c o n s t a n t
GCE/1@NGQDs showed the highest catalytic rate constant, Table 2, followed by GCE/NGQDs it can be observed that the catalytic rate improved up conjugation.

Two cobalt phthalocyanine molecules; a symmetrically substituted tetra-4-((1,3-bis(dimethylamino)propan-2-yl)oxy) phthalocyaninato cobalt (1) and an unsymmetrically substituted tris-4-((1,3-bis(dimethylamino)propan-2-yl)oxy) mono carboxyphenoxy phthalocyaninato cobalt (2) were successfully synthesised and conjugated onto nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots yielding 1@NGQDs and 2@NGQDs. The conjugation was achieved via non-covalent π-π stacking. The synthesis of the compounds and their respective conjugates was confirmed using numerous characterization techniques such as UV–Vis absorption, Raman spectroscopy, and dynamic light scattering. The compounds were used as catalytic probes in the electrochemical detection of dopamine using cyclic voltammetry. Stability studies were conducted for the probes and they proved to be stable. Where the lowest and favourable oxidation peak potential was obtained for GCE/1@NGQDs. A decrease in the peak potentials was observed upon conjugation. There was a decrease in the limit of detection upon conjugation, GCE/1@NGQDs lowest limit of detection.

Nobuhle Ndebele: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Pinar Sen: Data curation, Methodology. Tebello Nyokong: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) and Rhodes University.
